section_title: LIMIT ORDER ENTRY TECHNIQUES FOR LONG-TERM TRADERS
content:
Welcome back to Lesson 7.2 of the January 2017 content, focusing on using limit entry techniques for long
- term traders.

When buying with a limit order, similar to buy stops, the monthly and or weekly charts should suggest
institutional order flow will be seeking a PD Array above the daily market price.
- The daily chart must post a bearish candle.
- The daily chart must close the candle with a down close.
- It is not valid while the daily chart candle is trading and or forming.

section_title: BUY LIMIT PLACEMENT AND MARKET CONTEXT
content:
The buy limit is going to be placed at the bearish candle's close on the daily chart. We are looking to enter
a buy limit order at the close of a daily candle that's down close, while the market is bullish and we're
expecting a higher price move in the asset class we're trading.
- Markets will appear undervalued in an already primarily bullish market condition.

section_title: LEVERAGING MARKET STRUCTURE BREAKS FOR LONG-TERM ENTRIES
content:
This technique is preferably useful when the market has already shown the willingness to have a market
structure break and has started to move higher.
- While swing trading can utilize this for better fills closer to turning points, this module focuses on entering
between big turning points to still be a long-term trader and capitalize on significant pips.

section_title: DEEP DISCOUNT ENTRIES WITH BUY LIMIT ORDERS
content:
When using a buy limit entry at the close, we expect the very next day or next candle to open up and move
below that down candle's close.
- This means we are buying at a deeply undervalued price; it's already going to be oversold without
requiring any indicators.
- We are buying at a deep, deep discount when the market is predisposed to move higher.
- This technique nails down extremely awesome entry points, providing immediate feedback and almost
immediate profitability.

When selling with limit orders, similar to sell stops, the monthly and or weekly charts should suggest
institutional order flow will be seeking PD Arrays below the daily market price.
- The daily chart should post a bullish candle.
- The daily chart must close the candle with an up close.
- It is not valid while the daily chart candle is trading and or forming.

section_title: SELL LIMIT PLACEMENT AND MARKET CONTEXT
content:
The sell limit is going to be placed at the bullish candle's close on the daily chart. We are looking to have a
sell limit order right there at the candle's close.
- This means we are selling short in an overvalued or overbought condition in a market that's predisposed
to go lower based on monthly and or weekly PD Arrays that will draw price lower.

section_title: SELLING SHORT IN OVERVALUED CONDITIONS AGAINST INSTITUTIONAL FLOW
content:
Institutional flow should draw prices lower on the daily, but we are getting an up movement against what
longer-term institutional order flow suggests on monthly and or weekly charts.
- While price is moving higher for this particular day, we're looking to sell short at the next day, but it has to
be above this particular candle's close.

section_title: CRITICAL ALIGNMENT WITH DAILY PD ARRAYS
content:
We are not just selling on a limit on any up candle if we have an opinion about being bearish. These
conditions are best suited when we align them with a daily PD Array.
- This means looking for a bearish order block, a gap that it's traded into, a filled void, a trade above a
recent high, or a retreat back into an old historical low (support broken becomes resistance).
- Do not indiscriminately find up candles and down candles; the candle itself is not the be-all end-all system.
- You must blend the PD Arrays on the daily chart as well.

section_title: HIGH PROBABILITY ENTRIES AND THE JUDAS SWING
content:
By blending these conditions with the higher time frame monthly and weekly charts pulling price in one
direction (bullish or bearish), when we have opposing conditions on the daily, it creates low-risk, high
- probability entry patterns.
- For example, on monthly and weekly, if you're expecting lower prices due to bearish PD Arrays, you're
expecting to see up movement on the daily to go to a short-term premium.
- Selling short on a limit above that candle's close allows you to essentially get that last little piece of
market movement, that last little surge.
- Many times, in day trades, this is what you'll see as the 'Judas Swing' where it opens, makes the high in
London, and then sells off, becoming the beginning of a long-term move.

Let's revisit the Japanese Yen example using this idea. You can see the down candle and using its close as
our limit order to be a buyer.
- Each down candle's close, the next day, trades down below it, allowing you to be filled on your limit going
long.
- Observe the responsiveness of price immediately after that. There are five examples on this daily time
frame.

On the weekly chart, price was moving away from the 100 level. There was a bearish order block up
around the 118-119 level, which was the weekly premium PD Array expected to draw price up long term.
- Simultaneously, a bullish order block was identified down in the discount level at 100, showing a
willingness to bounce off that level.

section_title: DAILY ENTRIES WITHIN THE WEEKLY BULLISH ORDER BLOCK
content:
In November, during the post-election rally of Donald Trump, price stabbed one more time down into that
weekly bullish order block.
- Prior to that November dip, in September, we saw a rally away from a previous order block formed in
August 2016.
- In September, price retraced back down into it, and using the down candle, the very next day, it did trade
down below the close, giving an amazing fill.
- The lines drawn from the down candle up into the red shaded area represent the weekly PD Array or
bearish order block, indicating where price should be drawn in the weekly.

From the low formed in September, buying on a limit below the daily candle's close, the move all the way
up to the weekly PD Array or bearish order block accounted for 1800 pips for that particular low.

The next opportunity came around November 17th. A limit order below the daily close would have been
filled, and from that point up to the weekly PD Array (weekly bearish order block), it was a 980-pip move.
- The next down close on the daily chart, using its close as a buy limit order entry point, resulted in a 785
- pip move.
- Another down candle yielded a 600-pip rally.
- Another offered 500 pips.
- The last one, right before price reached the weekly PD Array, provided a 360-pip price move.

section_title: RECAP: DEEP DISCOUNT BUYING AND LONG-TERM OPPORTUNITIES
content:
Again, buying below the candle's close on a limit means you're buying that real deep, suppressed,
undervalued market.
- It's in a deep discount and will look to seek to move to a premium.
- This is an amazing concept to study on your charts, especially on daily time frames.

section_title: ABUNDANT OPPORTUNITIES ON HIGHER TIME FRAMES
content:
Many folks assume that because we're on higher time frame charts (not hourly or 15-minute charts), there
aren't many opportunities. While there aren't as many as day trades or scalps, there are plenty of
opportunities.
- When in long-term trends with a clear indication to move higher to a monthly or weekly level, there are
many opportunities to get positioned without needing to catch the exact high or low.
- Even beyond the equilibrium price point, there were many opportunities to gain several hundred pips.
Such numbers are very respectable and not to be scoffed at.
CONCLUSION:

Do you still think you need intraday trading to make pips? Hopefully, after this teaching, you'll quickly
conclude that you do not need it.
- For those who felt ICT only teaches day trading and short-term things, this is the type of trading where
you can find opportunities, maintain relatively low risk, and find a profitable framework for your trading
ideas to pan out.
- This example used just one pair and one focus on a PD Array on a weekly and monthly basis.
